1: The allocation of a holy district to YHWH when the land is divided — the sacred portion comes first in the ordering of the new land. A shadow: in the new creation, the holiness of God is the organizing center around which everything else is arranged. The new Jerusalem of Revelation 21 is organized around the throne of God and the Lamb — holiness at the center, all else radiating outward. Christ is the holy district around whom the new order of creation is structured.
2: A square sanctuary with open land around it — set apart by the perimeter. A shadow: the holiness of the sanctuary requires a buffer, a space between the holy and the common. In Christ, the barrier is not spatial but sacrificial — he is himself the buffer, the mediator who stands between YHWH's holiness and human uncleanness, absorbing the danger and enabling the approach.
3: The priests who draw near to minister receive the holy portion with the sanctuary in it. The geographic proximity of the priests to the sanctuary reflects their spiritual proximity to YHWH. A shadow: Christ is the high priest who "draws near to God" most intimately — "through him we both have access in one Spirit to the Father" (Ephesians 2:18). The priestly proximity of 45:3 becomes the universal access of the new covenant community in Christ.
4: The holy portion for the priests who minister in the sanctuary — they will have their houses there and a holy place for the sanctuary. Priests live adjacent to the sanctuary they serve. A shadow: the dwelling of the priests near the holy place anticipates the dwelling of Christ's people in the presence of God — "they will see his face" (Revelation 22:4). The new Jerusalem has no separate sanctuary because "its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb" (Revelation 21:22).
5: The Levites who serve in the temple receive a portion — twenty-five thousand cubits long and ten thousand wide, with dwelling places for them. A shadow: all who serve YHWH receive their dwelling from YHWH. Christ's promise — "In my Father's house are many rooms... I go to prepare a place for you" (John 14:2) — is the personal fulfillment of the Levitical dwelling allocation. The servant receives a place prepared by the Lord himself.
6: The city portion alongside the holy district — belonging to the whole house of Israel. The city of YHWH's people adjacent to the sacred precinct. A shadow: the new Jerusalem of Revelation 21 where the city and the dwelling of God are coextensive — "Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man" (Revelation 21:3). The spatial relationship between the holy district and the city is resolved in the new creation where the whole city is holy.
7: The prince receives land on both sides of the holy district — east and west. The messianic prince's inheritance flanks the sacred center. A type: Christ inherits on both sides of the holy — the peoples to the east (the Gentiles) and the peoples to the west (the nations of the Mediterranean world). "I will give you the nations as your inheritance and the ends of the earth as your possession" (Psalms 2:8). The prince's land on both sides of the sanctuary is the spatial image of the Messiah's universal inheritance.
8: "This shall be his possession in Israel. And my princes shall no more oppress my people, but they shall give the rest of the land to the house of Israel according to their tribes." The messianic prince does not oppress but distributes justly. A type: Christ exercises authority as servant, not oppressor — "I am among you as the one who serves" (Luke 22:27). The prince of 45:8 who gives rather than takes is the type of Christ who gives his life as a ransom for many (Matthew 20:28).
9: "Enough, O princes of Israel! Put away violence and oppression, and execute justice and righteousness." The divine demand for just leadership. A revelation of God: YHWH confronts exploitative power directly and demands justice. Christ is the fulfillment of this demand — "I have come that they may have life and have it abundantly" (John 10:10) is the anti-oppression mission of 45:9 enacted in person. The prince who puts away violence is the one who is himself put to violence in order to end the cycle.
10: Honest scales, an honest ephah, an honest bath — the basic requirements of commercial integrity. A revelation of God: YHWH's concern extends to the weights and measures of daily commerce. The holiness that the temple represents must pervade the marketplace. Christ's cleansing of the temple (John 2:13-17) addresses exactly this intersection of commerce and holiness — he drives out those who have turned the house of prayer into a market. Honest weights in 45:10 anticipate the honest dealings Christ demands of the kingdom community.
11: The ephah and bath as standard measures — fixed proportions of the homer. The standardization of measurement units. A revelation of God: consistency and fairness are expressions of divine character. YHWH does not shift standards — his measures are reliable. Christ as the "faithful and true witness" (Revelation 3:14) embodies this divine consistency: "Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever" (Hebrews 13:8). The standard that cannot be manipulated is the character of God expressed in Christ.
12: The shekel defined as twenty gerahs — a fixed monetary unit. Covenant economics requires reliable currency. A revelation of God: economic reliability is a covenant concern. The gospel economy operates on a different currency — grace, not earned merit — but with the same reliability. Christ's promises are the currency of the kingdom: "truly I say to you" functions as the divine guarantee that no shekel-standard can match.
13: The grain offering: one-sixth of an ephah from each homer of wheat. The regular, proportional contribution that sustains the temple worship. A shadow: the regular, proportional giving that sustains the community of worship is the outer form of what Christ embodies inwardly — his entire life is a grain offering, the daily bread broken and given. "I am the bread of life" (John 6:35) is the grain offering of 45:13 made personal and inexhaustible.
14: The oil contribution: one-tenth of a bath from each cor. Oil as the regular temple provision. A shadow: the oil of anointing in OT religion (for priests and kings) is the shadow of the Spirit's anointing. "The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me" (Luke 4:18) is the Christological realization of the oil-provision: the regular oil-offering becomes the permanent anointing of the Anointed One (Mashiach).
15: One sheep from every flock of two hundred — for grain offerings, burnt offerings, and fellowship offerings, for atonement. The sheep as the regular atonement provision. A type: the sheep from the flock is the most immediate type of Christ in the Levitical system. "Behold the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world" (John 1:29) — the lamb from the flock of 45:15 becomes, in Christ, the Lamb from God himself, provided by YHWH where Abraham's ram provided for Isaac (Genesis 22:8).
16: All the people are obligated to make this offering to the prince. The community's support of the prince's worship obligations. A type: Christ as the messianic prince makes the offerings on behalf of the community and the community supplies the materials. This is the inverse of how Christ's atonement works — he provides the offering himself while the community's "support" is simply to receive it. The obligation of 45:16 becomes the invitation of the gospel: "come to me" (Matthew 11:28).
17: "It shall be the prince's duty to provide the burnt offerings, grain offerings, and drink offerings at the feasts, the new moons, the Sabbaths, and all the appointed feasts of the house of Israel." The messianic prince bears the full liturgical provision for the community. A direct type: Christ as the one who "provides" in the most ultimate sense — he is both the priest who offers and the offering itself. "He offered up himself" (Hebrews 7:27); "For our sake he made him to be sin" (2 Corinthians 5:21). The prince who provides all the regular offerings at all the feast occasions is the type of the one who provides the once-for-all offering that fulfills every feast.
18: The new year begins with a sin offering for the sanctuary — a bull without blemish to cleanse the temple. A type: the annual sanctuary purification reflects the need for ongoing access-renewal. Christ's once-for-all offering supersedes this annual cycle: "He entered once for all into the holy places... securing an eternal redemption" (Hebrews 9:12). The annual bull that purifies the sanctuary becomes the single offering that purifies the conscience once for all.
19: The priest puts blood from the sin offering on the doorpost of the temple and the corners of the altar ledge. Blood on the doorpost recalls the Passover (Exodus 12:7) — the protective application of sacrificial blood. A type: the blood-doorpost is the Passover type; Christ is the ultimate Passover sacrifice whose blood "speaks a better word" (Hebrews 12:24) than even the annual sanctuary-renewal of Ezekiel 45:19.
20: "You shall do the same on the seventh day of the month for anyone who has sinned unintentionally or through ignorance." The atonement cycle covers both deliberate and inadvertent sin through the regular purification rituals. A revelation of God: YHWH's provision for atonement addresses the full spectrum of human failure, not just spectacular transgressions. Christ's atonement similarly covers the full range — "he is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world" (1 John 2:2).
21: "In the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month, you shall keep the Passover, a feast of seven days; unleavened bread shall be eaten." The Passover in the restored order — the feast of redemption. A direct type: Christ is "our Passover lamb" who "has been sacrificed" (1 Corinthians 5:7). The Passover that Ezekiel's vision inscribes into the new order is the feast that Christ fulfills at the Last Supper (Luke 22:15: "I have eagerly desired to eat this Passover with you before I suffer") and that the Lord's Table perpetuates as memorial feast until his return.
22: On Passover day the prince provides a bull as a sin offering for himself and all the people. The messianic prince participates in the atonement ritual on behalf of himself and the community. A type with a Christological distinction: unlike the Zadokite priest of Ezekiel 44:27, Christ does not need a sin offering for himself — "he who knew no sin" (2 Corinthians 5:21). The prince of 45:22 who offers for himself AND the people becomes, in Christ, the one who offers for the people alone, having no personal sin to atone for.
23: Seven bulls and seven rams without blemish as burnt offerings for each of the seven feast days. The completeness of the seven-day offering cycle. A shadow: the perfection of sevenfold offering points to the one perfect, complete offering of Christ. Hebrews contrasts the repetitive daily offerings of the Levitical priests with Christ's single offering that accomplished what no repetition could: "once for all" (Hebrews 10:10). The sevenfold perfection of 45:23 is fulfilled in the one offering that is intrinsically complete.
24: Grain offerings of one ephah per bull and per ram, with a hin of oil per ephah. The regular proportional provision accompanying the burnt offerings. A shadow: the grain and oil that accompany every major sacrifice are the ordinary gifts of creation that YHWH transforms into worship. Christ transforms the ordinary elements of bread and wine into the vehicle of the new covenant memorial — the grain-and-oil of 45:24 become the bread-and-cup of the Lord's Table.
25: The seventh-month feast (Tabernacles/Sukkot) mirrors the same offering pattern as Passover. The two great feasts of Israel's calendar — Passover and Tabernacles — bookend the agricultural and redemptive year. A shadow: Christ fulfills both feasts — Passover in his death (John 18:28; 1 Corinthians 5:7) and Tabernacles in his promised eschatological dwelling with his people (Revelation 21:3; John 7:37-38, where he teaches at Tabernacles and offers living water). The two-feast structure of Ezekiel 45:21-25 maps onto the two advents of Christ.
